date: 2026-06-05
video_id: 4QgXgaV60aE
url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4QgXgaV60aE
title: The Macro Minute: Should the Fed tighten monetary policy?

Happy Friday out there to you, 42 with your skipper here, Darius Dale, to present our macro minute for Friday, June 5th, 2026. As always, we'll start with the executive summary from today's Lead-Off Morning Note, so let's dive right in. Today's key macro question is, should the Fed tighten monetary policy? The short answer is yes, absolutely and unequivocally yes. In terms of analytical nuance, roughly about a month ago, we signaled here and with our friends on Bloomberg TV and radio that the Fed would cause problems in asset markets if they adjust policy in either direction because bonds would react poorly to easing and stocks would react poorly to tightening. Fast forward to today, it appears that the stocks reacting poorly to tightening scenario is upon us. The May jobs report strongly supported our resilient US economy and paradigm C, aka run-it-hot themes. It did not support our jobless recovery theme. In fact, the evidence was so disconfirming of our jobless recovery theme that we believe a new dynamic may be emerging and it will confuse the heck out of investors and policymakers alike. Specifically, there is strong evidence of a cyclical upturn in the labor market, which is the natural byproduct of our resilient US economy and paradigm C, aka run-it-hot themes. There is also strong evidence of a secular downturn in the labor market when we observe the historically wide and widening spread between the private sector job openings rate and the private sector hires rate, as well as the dramatic underperformance of private nonfarm payrolls growth relative to key leading indicators of employment. Moreover, this rapidly ascending and structurally elevated long-term unemployment rate signals there is a growing category of roles that are likely being replaced by AI. The Fed should downgrade the labor market and its reaction function to focus on containing inflation in an economy that is being running nominally hot, which is precisely what we called for last April when we were the first on Global Wall Street to notice the administration's pivot uh to paradigm C, aka run-it-hot, from paradigm B, aka cut the deficit. Moreover, the likely transitory pullback in productivity growth will make it difficult for any FOMC member to look through the massive aggregate demand shock from AI CapEx, especially amid a modestly tight labor market, according to NAEIRU, and a modestly overheating economy, according to the output gap. That labor force growth is projected to slow from a long-run mean of 1.4% to 0.2% in 2026 means the bulk of economic growth must come from productivity or the economy will increasingly overheat. All told, the Fed must begin a tightening cycle, according to both the data and our market-implied Fed neutral rate model. How Kevin Warsh accomplishes this while simultaneously pulling back on forward guidance and maintaining investor confidence and supporting AI CapEx because of its long-term disinflationary potential will be a needle-threading exercise for the ages. As always, I'll try wrap up with a question from our community here in the 42 macro dashboard. This one's titled Chinese liquidity tightening. Says, "Hi Darius, I recently became a member of your community and your guidance, which has been very helpful. Much appreciated. I was reading a note from Michael Howell, liquidity guy on Substack, and was mentioning uh, he was mentioning the weak uh, gold performance might have to do with Chinese tightening of liquidity. As you are the king of data, I was curious to hear your views. Also trying to understand implications across commodity resource complex." So, this is something we um, definitely covered a few weeks ago, I believe, in our uh, May 14th lead-off morning note, where we highlighted the pullback in uh, Chinese liquidity provision and how that was causing a crusting of the broader uh, global liquidity uh, uh, uh, uh, uh, impulse. Um, impulse is still positive, uh, but it's now starting to to get uh, decelerate, uh, if you will, uh, uh, partially as a function of this pullback in Chinese liquidity that we highlighted a few weeks ago. Uh, um, um, that may that may, you know, deepen. We'll see. Uh you know, we're kind of getting to some pretty uh important time horizons with regards to uh the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and the the energy supply shock. So, the next few months could be very interesting in terms of how central banks around the world and how private sectors around the world really start to throttle back uh demand uh and activity uh uh you know, as we're as we're in response to what could very well clearly be a extended closure of the Strait of Hormuz. So, uh we'll wrap it up there. Uh uh Darius Dale here presenting our Macro Minute for Friday, June 5th, 2026. Uh just before we go, I just want to highlight that we have our uh June Macro Scouting Report presentation uh being published this afternoon uh to 42 Macro members. Uh if you're not a member, uh definitely you're definitely going to want to see this presentation. Uh I can't think of a more critical juncture for at the economy and asset markets to be in. I mean, this is you know, I'm not certainly not bearish at all, but you know, the last time the [clears throat] just emotionally you need to having you know, I do hundreds of slides of research every week. Uh the last time where I felt like the the the the the the the themes that built the legacy positioning in markets were being challenged by data a jump conditions in data to this degree. The last time we saw that was the uh late 2021. So, again, not not bearish. I'm not bearish. You know, I still think this market is is is is going to bubble when you take a medium-term time horizon, but I think the next few months could be very interesting and if you don't have a handle on the six key macro cycles, uh particularly the monitor US monetary policy cycle and the jump condition that we may see in that, uh then you're definitely going to want to check out that presentation. So, uh we'll leave you with that. Uh everyone have a wonderful weekend. We'll catch back here on Monday. Cheers.
